questions.question: A 5 kg body moves with a velocity of 8 m/s. Calculate the work required to stop it.
questions.answer: KE = 1/2 × 5 × 8² = 160 J; Work required = −160 J (negative work to stop).

questions.question: A force F = 10 N acts at an angle 60° to the displacement 4 m. Find the work done.
A body of mass 2 kg falls from a height of 10 m. Calculate velocity just before impact. (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.answer: PE at top = mgh = 2 × 10 × 10 = 200 J; KE just before impact = 200 J → 1/2 × 2 × v² = 200 → v² = 200 → v ≈ 14.14 m/s.

questions.question: A body of mass 1 kg is projected vertically upward with 20 m/s. Find maximum height attained. (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.answer: KE initial = 1/2 × 1 × 20² = 200 J; At max height KE = 0 → PE = 200 J → mgh = 200 → h = 200 / (1×10) = 20 m.

questions.question: A 2 kg object slides down a frictionless incline of 5 m height. Calculate its speed at the bottom. (g = 10 m/s²)
A force of 50 N acts on a body at 30° to displacement of 6 m. Find work done.
A body of mass 5 kg is lifted vertically 10 m. Friction force 20 N acts downward. Calculate net work done. (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.answer: Work by lifting force = mgh = 5×10×10=500 J; Work against friction = 20×10=200 J; Net work = 500 − 200 = 300 J.

questions.question: A body of mass 2 kg moves with speed 5 m/s. Find kinetic energy and power if it stops in 2 s.
questions.answer: KE = 1/2 × 2 × 5² = 25 J; Power = Work / Time = 25 / 2 = 12.5 W.

questions.question: A 3 kg body falls freely from a height of 4 m. Find velocity just before hitting ground. (g = 10 m/s²)
A 2 kg object is moving at 6 m/s. Calculate work done to stop it.
questions.answer: KE = 1/2 × 2 × 6² = 36 J; Work required = −36 J.

questions.question: A body of mass 2 kg is moving with 10 m/s. It comes to rest in 5 s. Calculate average power exerted to stop it.
questions.answer: KE = 1/2 × 2 × 10² = 100 J; Power = Work / Time = 100 / 5 = 20 W.

questions.question: A 2 kg body moves at 8 m/s and collides with a wall to stop. Calculate work done by wall.
questions.answer: KE = 1/2 × 2 × 8² = 64 J; Work done by wall = −64 J.

questions.question: A force of 40 N moves an object along 5 m at 60° to displacement. Find work done.
A body of mass 10 kg falls from height 5 m. Calculate kinetic energy just before hitting the ground. (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.answer: PE = mgh = 10 × 10 × 5 = 500 J → KE at bottom = 500 J.

questions.question: A spring stores 50 J of energy when stretched. If released, what is the maximum kinetic energy of the body attached?
questions.answer: Maximum KE = 50 J (all potential energy converts to kinetic energy).

questions.question: A body of mass 2 kg is moving at 3 m/s. Work done to bring it to rest in 3 s. Find average power.
A 5 kg body is lifted 2 m high by a constant force. Friction force = 10 N. Net work done?
questions.answer: Work against gravity = 5×10×2=100 J; Work against friction = 10×2=20 J; Net work = 100−20=80 J.

questions.question: A 1 kg body is moving with 12 m/s. Kinetic energy lost when speed reduces to 6 m/s?
questions.answer: KE initial = 1/2×1×12²=72 J; KE final = 1/2×1×6²=18 J; KE lost = 72−18=54 J.

questions.question: Work done by a 50 N force to move a body 5 m vertically upwards against gravity of 10 N?
questions.answer: Work done against gravity = Force × distance = 50 × 5 = 250 J.

questions.question: A body of mass 2 kg moves up a frictionless incline 5 m long, height 2 m. Work done against gravity?
questions.answer: PE gain = mgh = 2 × 10 × 2 = 40 J.

questions.question: A 1 kg mass attached to spring is stretched storing 20 J. If released, find velocity of mass.
A body of mass 3 kg slides down frictionless incline from height 4 m. Speed at bottom?
If a body moves under a force opposite to motion, what is the sign of work done?
questions.answer: Negative work.

questions.question: A 2 kg body falls from height 5 m. Work done by gravity?
questions.answer: PE = mgh = 2×10×5=100 J → Work done by gravity = 100 J (positive).

questions.question: A body is moving horizontally under a force at 90° to displacement. Work done?
questions.answer: Zero, as force is perpendicular to displacement.
